---
title: "Day 2 - Scientific Progress and Modeling Integration"
format: html
---

::: top-banner
:::

::: text-center
*Tuesday, June 9, 2026*
:::

## Objective

Review the scientific and modeling progress made since the 2025 workshop, identify what is ready to move forward, clarify the most important gaps and uncertainties, and gather targeted feedback to help strengthen the work over the next year.

# Session 1: Welcome and framing of progress since 2025 (09.00 - 10.30)

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
- **Welcome from Charles Darwin Foundation**  
  <a href="https://example.com" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Rakan Zahawi</a> or <a href="https://example.com" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">María José Barragán</a>  
  Brief welcome to Galápagos and the hosting facility.

- **Opening remarks**  
  <a href="https://isaakbm.github.io/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Isaac Brito-Morales</a>, <a href="https://www.conservation.org/experts-list/dr-lee-hannah" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Lee Hannah</a>  
  Brief welcome and overview of the workshop goals and expected outcomes.

- **Participant introductions**  
  Short introductions to align expertise across participants and ongoing contributions to the project.

- **Overview of progress since the 2025 workshop**

- **Key achievements**  
  Summary of major milestones reached across data, modeling, and coordination efforts.

- **Current status of the project**  
  Where we stand today across workstreams and collaborations.

- **Goals for Day 2**  
  Build a shared understanding of the progress made since the 2025 workshop, identify the most important scientific and technical gaps, and generate focused feedback to strengthen the work over the coming year.

- **Goals for the workshop**  
  Strengthen the scientific foundation of the project while helping ensure that its outputs are relevant, actionable, and useful for regional coordination, conservation planning, and policy-oriented decision-making.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

:::: text-center
::: section-small
*Coffee break (10.30 - 11.00)*
:::
::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Session 2: Scientific and modeling advances since 2025 (11.00 - 13.00)

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
This session presents key advances in climate vulnerability assessment, physical modeling, biological datasets, and species distribution modeling across the project. Together, these components help define both what has already been achieved and where focused improvements are needed to strengthen the next phase of work.

As you listen, please note which findings seem most useful for conservation planning, regional coordination, or policy-relevant decision-making, and what additional information would make these outputs more actionable. These reflections will help inform the breakout discussions later in the workshop.

- **Climate change vulnerability assessment**  
  <a href="https://www.conservation.org/experts-list/dr-lee-hannah" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Lee Hannah</a>, presented by <a href="https://example.com" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Sarayu Ramnath</a>  
  Overview of the assessment framework, datasets, and initial results across taxa.

- **Physical modeling and downscaling framework**  
  <a href="https://isaakbm.github.io/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Isaac Brito-Morales</a> *(including outcomes from Toulouse 2025)*  
  Summary of oceanographic datasets, downscaling approaches, and integration with biological data. Related workflow materials are available in the <a href="https://github.com/IsaakBM/cesmle-ocn-fetch" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">CESM Large Ensemble ocean downscaling repository</a>.

- **Status of biological data for species distribution modeling (SDMs)**  
  <a href="https://isaakbm.github.io/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Isaac Brito-Morales</a>, <a href="https://example.com" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Sarayu Ramnath</a>  
  Current state of compiled datasets, data gaps, and readiness for modeling.

- **Species modeling updates**

  These updates present preliminary to intermediate results and are intended to guide the next phase of model improvement. Given the project timeline, the goal is to identify the most important and realistic improvements needed to strengthen the final outputs. Participants are encouraged to focus feedback on what is most important to improve over the next year.

    1. Dolphins - <a href="https://www.linkedin.com/in/mario-a-pardo-cicese/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Mario Pardo</a>, <a href="https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=NE7puCcAAAAJ&hl=es" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Ariadna Juarez</a>  
       Progress on species-specific models and ecological insights.
    2. Mesopelagic systems - <a href="https://people.miami.edu/profile/4fd08a9092e08f6da5b330184ade8d86" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Yulia Egorova</a>  
       Advances in modeling midwater systems and implications for broader ecosystem understanding.
    3. First generation of SDMs - <a href="https://isaakbm.github.io/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Isaac Brito-Morales</a>  
       Presentation of initial model outputs, performance, and limitations.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

:::: text-center
::: section-small
*Lunch break (13.00 - 14.30)*
:::
::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Session 3: Synthesis across taxa and systems (14.30 - 16.00)

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
This session integrates insights across species, models, and environmental drivers to build a regional perspective. It will begin with a brief synthesis of key patterns and emerging insights, followed by small-group discussion to reflect on what is most relevant, what remains unclear, and how these findings could be used in practice.

- **Cross-taxa patterns and emerging insights**  
  Identification of shared patterns across species and functional groups.

- **Key environmental drivers and habitat use**  
  Discussion of dominant physical and biogeochemical drivers shaping distributions.

- **Integration of biological and physical processes**  
  Linking species responses to oceanographic structure and variability.

- **Discussion**

    - What are we learning at the regional scale?
    - What are the main knowledge gaps or uncertainties?
    - Which findings seem most relevant for conservation, planning, or regional coordination?
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

:::: text-center
::: section-small
*Coffee break (16.00 - 16.30)*
:::
::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Session 4: Strategic discussion and next steps (16.30 - 17.00)

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
- **Priority gaps to address**  
  Key data and modeling limitations that require immediate attention.

- **Short-term actions and next steps**  
  Concrete steps to advance analyses before the next milestone.

- **Alignment across teams and workstreams**  
  Ensuring coordination across taxa, models, and regional efforts.

- **Preparation for upcoming project milestones**  
  Positioning outputs to feed into future projections and decision-making processes.
:::
